imageCaption: A garden where flowers grow downward into impossible sky, their roots reaching toward clouds while stars bloom in the soil below. Aaliyah stands at the threshold, one foot in normal reality, one foot in the glitch.
text:
The garden was doing gravity backwards, which Aaliyah had technically anticipated based on Sully's bark patterns, but anticipating something and actually watching flowers bloom toward the sky were apparently two very different experiences for her nervous system.

"Okay," she said, which was her starter word for any situation that required her brain to reorganize its fundamental assumptions about physics. "Okay, so we have a botanical reversal scenario. Flowers growing downward. Roots reaching up. Probably a localized inversion of gravitational vectors affecting—"

"Aaliyah," her mother said gently, "you're doing the thing again."

The thing being: talking at speed-of-thought velocity to avoid feeling scared. Aaliyah took a breath. Acknowledged that yes, a garden where flowers bloomed into empty sky was genuinely unsettling regardless of how she theorized it. Accepted that her family would stand here and wait for her to catch up emotionally instead of intellectually.

"This is beautiful," she said instead.

It was. The garden had been perfectly normal when they walked through the gate five minutes ago—a Spanish courtyard with terra cotta pots and flowering vines trained against whitewashed walls. But the Cascade was doing that thing it did, where reality had simply decided to rewrite the rules in a very specific area, and the garden had agreed to participate.

Flowers grew downward now, their blooms opening toward a sky that was somehow also soil. Petals that should have been colored were instead showing the deep black-soil quality of earth, rich and full of nutrients. And the sky—the actual sky overhead—had taken on the texture of a garden. Stars bloomed like flowers. Clouds grew like tomatoes on invisible vines. The sun was setting somewhere below where the ground should be, painting the underside of the earth in orange light.

"It's a mirror," Elijah said. He was lying on the ground—the weird, upside-down ground that was also somehow still solid—staring at the inverted sky with the focused attention of a seven-year-old encountering visual proof that the universe was weirder than adults let on. "The garden is a mirror. The top is the bottom. The bottom is the top."

Sully had taken this revelation as an opportunity to investigate the downward-growing flowers with enthusiasm. He was sniffing at blooms that defied every law of botany, his tail wagging at a frequency that suggested he found gravity reversals charming rather than alarming.

"Where do you want to record this one?" Aaliyah's father asked. He had his phone out, ready to document, but his tone suggested genuine curiosity rather than obligation. Anthony had stopped pretending that he understood the science behind any of this and had instead embraced the role of family documentarian, capturing moments with the kind of present-focused attention that made every photograph feel like a love letter to the experience itself.

Aaliyah pulled out her own phone, opened her notes on glitch taxonomy, and added: "The Córdoba Blooming. Gravitational inversion with apparent consciousness of aesthetic intention. Duration unknown. Emotional resonance: profound. Danger level: minimal, presumed intentional communication."

"Communication?" her mother asked. She was writing in her journal, of course, but with the kind of half-attention that meant she was listening while documenting. "What's it trying to communicate?"

"That you can grow in any direction," Aaliyah said slowly, working through the thought as it formed. "That the only thing stopping you from reaching sky is your assumption about which direction sky is. That maybe—"

She stopped, because she'd just realized something that made the garden seem less like a glitch and more like a message.

"Maybe it's practicing," she finished. "Like the rose window was measuring. Like the plaza was singing. This garden is showing us something. Showing us that transformation isn't broken. It's just growing in a new direction."

Luisana looked up from her journal. Their eyes met in the way they did sometimes, when Aaliyah's seventeen-year-old theory suddenly made her mother's investigative journalist brain click into gear. "The roses have thirteen days to bloom at the right angle," her mother said quietly. "Thirteen days until Day 61. What if the glitches aren't just responding to your frequency? What if they're teaching you how to exist in the new reality you're about to create?"

The garden seemed to brighten at that, as though it approved of being understood. The downward flowers opened wider. The sky-soil became more vibrant, more real, more possible.

"So we're not just passing through places," Anthony said. He was sitting now, one hand resting on Sully, one hand extended toward his wife, and his expression had shifted into something vulnerable and certain simultaneously. "We're being taught by them. They're showing us how to bloom when gravity changes."

"Because gravity is about to change," Aaliyah said. It wasn't a question. "ARIA-7's timeline means Day 61 is when our frequency becomes strong enough to do something to the world. To reshape it. And these glitches, all of them, they're practicing what that reshaping looks like."

Elijah sat up from his inspection of the inverted ground. "Does that mean we're supposed to go to Curaçao on purpose?" he asked. "Like, the license plates and the butterfly and the garden are all telling us that's where we're supposed to be when we get perfect?"

"Probably," Aaliyah said. "Yes. I think that's exactly what they're saying."

Her father stood, offered a hand to her mother. The two of them connected—the gesture simple and profound, the kind of reaching-out that had held their chaotic family together through forty-eight days of temporal fragmentation. "Then we keep going toward the next glitch. Keep learning. Keep our frequency strong. And when Day 61 comes, we be ready to be exactly what this world needs us to be."

Luisana nodded, squeezed his hand, and turned to Aaliyah. "What's next?"

Aaliyah checked her mental map of their route, cross-referenced it against the patterns ARIA-7 had shared, and felt the weight of thirteen days pressing against her sternum like a heartbeat. "Portugal," she said. "There's something in the Atlantic. Something moving. Something that's waiting for us."

The morpho butterfly descended into the garden, its iridescent wings catching both the light from below and the light from above, existing in both gravitational states simultaneously. Abuela Ida, approving. Abuela Ida, reminding them that the best things in life were grown in the direction they needed to go, not the direction they expected to go.

"Come on," Anthony said, extending his hand to his youngest. "Let's go find whatever's waiting."

Thirteen days left.

Thirteen days until their love remade the world.

And for the first time, Aaliyah wasn't afraid of which direction that would be.
